title: Mo Jahama -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Mo Jahama -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: Good, you're muted.
Sorry about that.
Hey, Andy, how are you?
Good, how are you?
Well, I'd like Wednesday because you're almost done with the week.
How about you?
Yes, likewise.
Well, it's been a short week, so extra treats.
Are you in the U.S.
or where are you located?
Yeah, I'm located in Jersey City.
Oh, awesome.
How about you?
I'm also on the East Coast.
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh, I like Rhode Island.
I used to go to Providence a lot.
It's really pretty.
Yeah, it's a perfect, I think it's the perfect size city.
It's like just big enough, but not too big.
Lots of good food, too.
Yeah, I want to go back, honestly, because it's like kind of like, I don't know, not a town, but not as overwhelming as a city like you were saying.
Yeah, yeah.
Uh, well, it's not too far from you, so it's an easy train ride.
Yeah, hopefully, hopefully.
just need to, like, get a little time off work, you know?
Yeah.

Uh, well, speaking of, um, I, uh, we have 30 minutes, so I want to make sure we make the most of it, um, so I can give you a little bit of background on the role, growthx, um, and, and then, um, talk about you, and then I'll try and leave, like, five, ten minutes at the end for you to ask questions.
Sure, sounds good.
Cool.

Um, so I lead customer operations at GrowthX, which just means I'm thinking about, like, people, processes, how those all come together, uh, the tools we use, how it all comes together to, uh, put together our deliverables for our clients.
Um, and so I lead, essentially, the delivery org, which is kind of the customer-facing, um, side of our business.
GrowthX at its heart is a services company, um, so we...
have our own underlying tech that runs AI workflows to generate all the content that we're creating, that we're putting out.
But what we, you know, one of the things that sets us apart is the human in the loop for like that AI content production.
And so this role is kind of the core of our pod.
So we structure our delivery org in pods.
A managing editor manages three to five clients and works alongside between one and three content managers who are actually running the underlying workflows to generate the content.
The managing editor is doing a couple of things after that content gets like produced as a first draft.
So obviously there's like an editorial aspect, that's, you know, making sure that

That the tone, brand, voice, all those things are aligned, like, thinking about, like, if it's a technical product, like, how we want to check that and approval processes, checking against kind of any artifacts or content that they've provided us for accuracy.

And then on the other side, project managing kind of at a higher level, like, are we producing the amount of content that we said we were going to produce at the level that we said we were going to produce it?
That's working with the client, letting them know kind of where we are on the roadmap.

And then as part of kind of, like, that editing and project management piece, as you're editing, you should get a sense for what's working and not working and what we can improve in terms of the feedback loop for our product team that's building the actual workflows that generate the content.
So, So, yeah.

That should have, like, some sort of, like, obviously, AI knowledge and curiosity, some technical ability in terms of kind of expressing what you think should change based on kind of what you've seen in the output of the content.
So those are the, like, three kind of pieces that are involved in the technical managing editor role.
The other person that works kind of over the pods is an account strategist or account director.
That role is really thinking kind of long-term about, you know, what are the goals that we set out to achieve together with our clients?
How are we thinking about, you know, pivoting on strategy if things aren't working?
What are our analytics telling us about where we are in terms of, like, getting clients to the KPIs?
that they said are important, and helping kind of set the long-term roadmap for client partnerships.
So that's what the delivery org looks like.
That's what the role looks like.
Do you have any questions?
So I guess it's specifically, like, is there a specific kind of content?
Are you just generating content for growth?
Is it, is there, like, different clients have different needs?
Or is there a specific kind of, you know, content you're willing to generate or hoping to generate?
Yeah, we think about, like, editorial blog content is kind of our bread and butter at the moment, generating five to ten pieces per week for clients.
And then the other side of that is PSEO content.
So if we're developing a template where, you know, we can do, like, versus pages of product comparisons or glossary pages with, like, a lot of definitions, things that are kind of really easy to.
Generate at scale using AI, we'll run those where we're generating like 25 to 100 pieces.
then also where we have clients that have a ton of content, we'll do content refreshes.
Like we have some clients that have like over a thousand blog posts.
And so that's just like making sure that everything's like still ranking and relevant and crawlable by AI, things like that.
Awesome.
That's really cool.
Yeah, I mean, that sounds like right off my alley, just because, you know, I've been working on content generation systems basically for the past year.
And I'm having a lot of fun with AI just because it allows me to build prototypes to show to clients rather than PowerPoint presentations.
And I think that just has allowed me to really impress clients with a front end that shows them exactly how content is generated, what kind of analytics they can expect.
And I just love the magic.
Of LLMs and AI in general, I'm such a big like tech geek.
So for me, like I worked on video content generation recently, and it's just magical.
Like just the idea that I can talk to this computer, and it comes back to me in little worlds that it imagines or just intelligent responses.
Like, we've moved, we've moved so much, there's been so much progress, and it's happening so fast.
And I love keeping up with it.
But yeah, I've worked a lot with coding tools as well.

So just seeing, I guess, the strengths that these models have been able to get better at, and finding a pipeline, you know, like leveraging multiple models, plan templates, and then build prompts for other models, and chain them together to kind of generate as reliable content as possible.
I worked recently on like lessons.
Content generation for an edutech.

It was a lot of fun just figuring out how we can go through their database, find granular lesson concepts that allow the bots to build lessons, build quizzes, build feedback for the lessons, and then, you know, doing QA with real teachers, building a system for that.
And, yeah, I've just had a lot of fun with AI lately, and just seeing how it can streamline marketing so effectively.
That's amazing.
Well, so I can see that you're freelancing now, and I'm sure you've got some, it sounds like you've got some super interesting projects.
I'm curious, like, what you're looking for, and what's exciting about this role?
I'm kind of looking to leverage LLMs in a way where I can take advantage of my technical expertise, but at the same time apply creativity, just because...

I think, you I think the future is going to be pretty interdisciplinary, and like the most key skills are going to be critical reading, critical thinking, and learning how to kind of direct these LLMs or agents, basically, to be as effective as possible.

Because they can really streamline work in a very effective way, but simultaneously, you have to like identify, you know, human emotional intelligence, because they don't really know what exactly is going on, or how to explain to clients, like, this is what we're giving you.

And this is how this can drive revenue, or drive clicks, or drive engagement, which I think is really interesting, and just figuring out how to optimize SEO, like for articles, is something I've done a lot of in the past, and kind of trying to rank, I worked a lot with SEMrush for my last full-time job, and made a lot of SEO presentations.

And there was like a really interesting, so I worked for Helium Mobile, and there was a real big problem we had where we would type in, is Helium Mobile legit, which is a big issue for critical companies.
And the first thing that would come up is this Reddit poster who was so angry at Helium Mobile because he wasn't generating income from like hosting their hotspots.
And like, we had to write so much content to get rid of that.
We had to build a Google business profile, which they didn't even have at the time.
And just like figuring out creative ways to actually show off the product as opposed to, you know, relying on user reviews that can be negative because they're polarizing in the crypto world.
And, you know, it's, it's been a lot of fun, I think, working across industries and figuring out, okay, well, we can, we can use AI, but how do we use it in a way that leverages our creativity as a.

As to replacing it, and I think it's really interesting what you said about building templates, because, you know, you find a lot of the time that people are doing everything so manually, and having templates that, you know, leverage CSS, or sometimes even leverage Python, like, can streamline work so quickly instead of typing everything out individually, yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Awesome.
Well, I mean, your experience is clear and so interesting.
Like, I think you could, we could leverage a lot of, like, what you've done, and sounds like you'd, I think you'd like to see our pipelines too.
I would love to, yeah.
very interesting.
I'll let, I'll let Panzer show those to you when you chat with him.
I do want to talk a little bit about, like, project management and kind of working with multiple clients, because that is the other side of it, right?
Is like that juggling piece.
So can you talk to me about kind of how you
You have worked with multiple clients at once, and your organization and prioritization.
Yeah, sure.

I mean, when I started my career, it was with a search agency where I worked with PayPal, I worked with Four Seasons, I worked with SAP, and finding out exactly what each client is looking for involves a lot of competitive analysis, and a lot of, you know, what are the KPIs that are most effective?
What can drive revenue immediately?
What can drive engagement for that specific industry?
And I think that's where I've had a lot of interesting experiences just figuring out, okay, like, what does this client need that they don't know they need right now?
Looking through data and trying to find gaps in content.
One of the things I worked on initially was it was called like keyword provided algorithm, which is not the best name, but we would find gaps in like...
Huge enterprises, content, and approach them and say, okay, this can rank immediately.
And I remember working with Godiva, and just finding really interesting gaps, where like, you know, most of their sales are on Valentine's Day, or Monday.
And it was so quick, like, it was just my own cultural experience, which is like, I go to Dubai, and Godiva chocolates are premium there.
Like, that's the first thing they serve.
And we were able to just make a few, a few, like, pages about Ramadan and rank immediately, and be top search result.
So I think it's just kind of, working with multiple clients is more fun than working with one.
At Helium Mobile, like, most of their revenue is from big B2B clients, where they can save a lot of money by hosting through Helium Mobile.
They can save money on internet, they can save money on business cell phone plans.
And I would have to work on content that like, could work for multiple clients and sometimes actually talk to them about what they need.
And I think also just with Seeking Alpha, like they have investing groups, so I'd have to talk to different investing experts about what exactly they're looking for.
So it's just something I feel like I've done a lot of in the past.

And I love Trello, I love Todoist, I love Notion, just anything that allows me to brain dump so that I don't get too nervous about approaching a client, don't get too nervous about moving from one client to another.
And I think it's kind of about learning and talking to someone and seeing, okay, this is what they need, this is what they'd like to hear.
And finding this balance between technical specifications and kind of emotional intelligence has worked really well for me.
Also trying to stay calm, you know?
Yeah, yeah.
Nothing is the end of the world.
Great advice.
Yeah, exactly.
Cool, cool.
Well, I mean, those are good examples.
Thanks for talking me through kind of some of the clients you've worked with.
Very interesting.
All right.
Those are all the questions I had.
What questions do you have for me?
I'm curious, like, because I saw you work with Webflow and Reddit and a bunch of startups.
Like, how, what are the different goals that clients have with their content?
Because you said some of these clients already have thousands of blog posts.
I imagine other clients don't have that many posts.
And so how do you, like, move between them?
And what goals does each client have generally?
Yeah, well, so I think that's an interesting question right now, because we didn't say no to anybody for the first year.
And now we're in, I guess, year two, technically.
year technically.
we're two,
Like our existence.
And we're starting to really hone the definition of our ICP and what we want to do for clients.
So there was a period where sky was the limit.
And we'd like, you know, try and build a custom pipeline for every client.
That's just not scalable.
Um, we, we do find more success with clients that have some presence established because a lot of what we're trying to move the needle on is like SEO, like search rankings.
And, um, and then obviously there's a lot of curiosity right now on LLM visibility and, um, and reference points.
Um, so are you being cited?
Are you coming up in answers when somebody asks questions to the LLMs?
So, um, I think those are the two big goals, obviously.
SEO expertise.
Yeah, go ahead.
Did I?
sorry.
You just cut out for a second.
And I, those
Those are the big goals.
And then you cut out into SEO expertise.
Sorry.
Oh, yeah.
So geo, like geo is what we call it, generative engine optimization, and then SEO expertise.
So that's, that's really what clients are coming to us for.

And I think we don't want to, like, we certainly have clients where there's zero blog content, zero anything, but the, the understanding of the, the like time it takes to build up a web presence and like, establish credibility and domain authority.
We want somebody that understands that.
Sometimes like super early stage companies don't have that, like patience or understanding of what it takes.
So we're looking for people who have like done it before or seen how hard it is to do.
So, um, and can give us like some trust and leeway as we're building because it's not going to be.
You know, a one-day solution.
Yeah, yeah.
I think what I found interesting is, like, they're still using basically the same algo for, like, generated content.
I know, like, I gave a presentation about this with Helium Mobile.
Like, what was the acronym?
E-E-A-T.
So, like, Google's really focusing on user reviews and on trying to think user content more so than, like, corporate content.
And so you have to, like, find this balance between, you know, being really formal and looking for SEO ranking while simultaneously being, you know, tackling how does this affect the user?
Because oftentimes, like, you'll find that they're citing from Reddit so much.
Yeah.
And it can, like, overwhelmingly shifted towards Reddit or towards Instagram.
And I think that can be easily combated, or not easily, but just by having responses to the things people are asking on social media.
you.
As opposed to, you know, letting users flood the, you know, reach the floodgates immediately.
But I am interested in seeing how these generative models are going to affect search.
Because everything now, when you search for it, the first thing that comes up is a very long response from Gemini.
And then you don't really hit the, you don't really hit the other search results until you scroll way down.
So, like, I'm seeing a lot of brands just have, use paid ads a lot more.
Just because it allows them to show up first.
And, like, users don't have the patience, really, to scroll all the way down sometimes.
Yeah.
Well, I guess the question is, you know, are people going to go back to Google searching with Gemini results or not?
And, yeah.
And then can you rank with Gemini?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I do think it's the same algorithm.
Like, I think they're using the same things that they used in search initially.
Like, you know, before Gemini was the first thing that popped up, and like, you can optimize for the same way you would optimize for SEO.
But I'm curious about that.
What have you found in regards to Geo versus SEO?
I mean, it's such a black box.
Like, we kind of know what's happening with Geo.
Like, you know how to game the system with SEO.
So, Geo is like, we've got, like, a list of best practices.
Can we show that those best practices are going to drive X amount of, like, you know, going to improve your presence by 3% or whatever?
Not necessarily.
Right now, we're just finding the best ways to track it so that we can try and look for signals and keep experimenting.
We are playing around with things like some.
Our clients are trying to, like, really be more active in conversations in places like Reddit and, like, service themselves as answers.
We're leaning into the LLM's TXT files right now because everybody's doing it.
And so you might as well see if it does something, even though, like, no LLM has, like, confirmed or denied that they're actually, like, referencing those files.
So I think, like, we don't want to discount anything.
And it's, it is hard, hard messaging to deliver to say, like, look, we want to try a bunch of things.
We're running experiments on, like, you know, you can add a button that's, like, summarize this page with ChatGPT.
Like, we're trying that.
We're trying, like, to just put, like, TLDR at the top of every page because it seems.
Like, LLMs are lazy.
It's, like, what I've heard.
So, you know, we're, like, let's do these things and see if we can improve our ranking.
And right now, it's just tracking and seeing if we can find signals that, like, where we can lean in or do more.
And that's it.
Like, but that's the only thing that you can do because it's such a black box.
But everybody's panicking about it.
And they're willing to pay anyone to, like, try.
And so, you know, we don't want to make false promises.
But we are being very proactive about what we're trying.
Yeah, yeah.
I found that, like, users are asking a lot more questions to the LLMs than they would, than they would initially, like, with just SEO.
So, finding ways to, like, have visible responses that are on brand that, you know, give users the answers they're looking for.
And, like, you know, sometimes that the LLMs will cite something and figuring...
How to be the first citation is, is interesting.
It's just so fun to think, okay, this is a new world.
And like, how do we figure this out?
Yeah, well, I mean, the other question is like, nobody, is anybody clicking into citations or mentions the only thing that matter?
And then, you know, the, oh, gosh, what were you just saying about?
Oh, yeah.
So I think the other thing that we're really recommending right now is like finding super niche problems or content.

So rather than being like, AI cybersecurity or something like, you know, and this is kind of an SEO tactic in general, but I think AI makes it much more relevant to say like, I, like, my specific role, trying to prevent, like, X kind of attack, you know, because that's what people are searching.
Searches and, or questions and LLMs are so much more, often so much more specific.
if you can.
Create content towards this specific problem that you're solving or pain point that you know that your high intent customer has, then you will get to the top.
But that is a lot more nuanced and also requires you to have, you can't just create that niche content because then you're not ranking for anything and you have just like five niche pages out there.
Yeah, I guess it involves a lot of understanding of like the brand and audience personas.
And yeah, I am interested to see like the workflows and how quickly does content generate?
Like, do you adapt each one for each brand?
You were saying it used to be customized to each brand and now I guess it's more templated.
Yeah, I mean, we have like the workflow is, you know, five, seven steps and then we can change the prompts within each step.
But...

You know, it's the same seven-ish steps, you know, we're going to the same LLM for the same, for like, you know, same platform for this step versus this step, know, perplexity is better at X, Claude is better at Y.

Um, and so, you know, this is the kind of output that we want, now let's just tweak the prompts within that, like, those seven steps, um, rather than, I think, like, doing really broad, or really, like, varied kinds of output, um, we're starting to standardize the kind of content that we're creating.
Yeah, yeah, because it seems like they're ranking content that kind of sounds like them.
Are you seeing that at all?
And, like, I'm wondering, like, one, because more and more content is being generated, and so, like, there's got to be a way to be that.
Niche answer, rather than hoping that, like, your generated content ranks above someone else's generated content.
And, like, I guess brand awareness is really interesting because you're really working on the whole funnel and seeing what exactly are customers looking for before they click.

I remember, like, the first company I worked with was focused entirely on search and on, like, figuring out the intention of the customer before they have that intention, in a way, and, like, what they're willing to spend and what they're willing to click on.
I'm just really fascinated with, like, okay, how do you optimize for this?
And I'm seeing that it's a big experiment, and I'm wondering, is it standardized at this point?
Is it easier?
You said it's easier with larger brands that already have content.
Yeah.
No, I, it's.
Definitely not standardized.
Like, and we're still, to some extent, like using Claude outside the platform in places where it's not, the results aren't perfect.
We're experimenting with more agentic type workflows.

So yeah, there's a lot of places where, and I think this is one of the interesting opportunities in this role, is like, looking at that output, looking at what the client is trying to do, and connecting those dots.
Because like the content manager is just generating the content.
And I mean, there's so much to generate and edit that I think they're thinking at like a very, like production, individual production level.
And so the technical managing editor, we want somebody that can think kind of, okay, what, this is what the client wants, and what they're trying to do is like, where's the workflow missing the mark?
And what kind of feedback can we, like, what can we ask from, in terms of engineering?
of engineering?
To change the prompts or change, like, the steps to get what we want.
So, like, there's customization involved in every pipeline we build, but there's kind of a baseline template that we have.
So it's not set it and forget it in any way, shape, or form.
Yeah, yeah.
I found that a lot, like, working freelance, is that autonomy is not there.
Like, even if you build the right prompts, like, you have to constantly iterate and optimize.
How is it, like, working in such a new kind of nuanced field that's really evolving so quickly?
Yeah, mean, I'm not in the day-to-day.
I'm more thinking about the, like, you know, how everybody's working as a whole.
I think some days it's very frustrating.
I'll be very honest.
Sometimes people get very frustrated because the output isn't what you need it to be, and we are producing at scale.
But I think, like, the people that are curious and willing to work with it, the...
And I think the skill of our engineering team right now makes it really satisfying when you do solve a problem, and I think there's just so much green space.
Unfortunately, we are at time, and I do have to go, but it was really great talking to you.
Definitely, I'll have you chat with the next person, but thank you so much for your time, Mo.
Awesome.
Really nice meeting you.
Thank you for your time.
Take care.
Have a good one.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview with Mo Jahama for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=252.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves editorial oversight, project management, and AI workflow optimization for content generation](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=330.0)
  - [Mo has extensive experience in AI-driven content creation, SEO, and client management across various industries](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=627.0)
  - [GrowthX focuses on editorial blog content (5-10 pieces/week) and SEO content (25-100 pieces) using AI-powered workflows](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=569.0)
  - [Company is refining its ICP and moving towards more standardized content creation processes](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1190.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview

  - [Technical Managing Editor manages 3-5 clients within a pod structure](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=382.0)
  - [Responsibilities include:](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=499.0)
      - [Editorial oversight (tone, brand voice, technical accuracy)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=414.0)
      - [Project management (content production, client communication)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=442.0)
      - [AI workflow optimization (feedback loop with product team)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=478.0)
  - [Works alongside 1-3 content managers who run underlying AI workflows](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=391.0)

### GrowthX Services and Content Types

  - [Primary focus: Editorial blog content (5-10 pieces/week) and SEO content (25-100 pieces)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=569.0)
  - [Additional services:](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=587.0)
      - [Content refreshes for large existing content libraries (1000+ blog posts)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=607.0)
      - [Template-based content generation (e.g., product comparisons, glossary pages)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=587.0)
  - [Emphasis on human-in-the-loop approach for AI content production](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=368.0)

### Mo's Experience and Interest

  - [Extensive work with AI content generation systems and LLMs](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=627.0)
  - [Experience in building prototypes and client-facing demos](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=636.0)
  - [Strong background in SEO, content strategy, and working across various industries](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=968.0)
  - [Keen interest in leveraging technical expertise with creativity in AI applications](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=769.0)

### Client Goals and Challenges

  - [Main client objectives: Improve SEO rankings and LLM visibility/citations](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1230.0)
  - [Varying client needs based on existing content and understanding of content marketing](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1177.0)
  - [Challenges in educating clients on realistic timelines for building web presence](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1208.0)
  - [Experimentation with new tactics for generative engine optimization (GEO)](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1451.0)

### Content Generation Process

  - [Standardized 5-7 step workflow with customizable prompts for each client](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1730.0)
  - [Utilization of different AI models (e.g., Claude, Perplexity) based on specific strengths](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1739.0)
  - [Ongoing experimentation and optimization of workflows](https://fathom.video/share/67ixoskgcaBT1MBxS5BYBUp4KjgCcb-c?tab=summary&timestamp=1871.0)
